questions.question: A 64-layer transformer with d=8192, L=8192, batch=1 in BF16 accumulates roughly 51 GB of activations — more than a single A100 GPU. What fundamental tradeoff does gradient checkpointing exploit to bring this under control?
options: It trades model size for activation size — smaller weight matrices require fewer intermediates
It trades compute FLOPs for activation memory — store only segment boundary inputs and recompute intermediates during backward instead of keeping them in GPU memory from the forward pass
It trades precision for memory — downcast activations to INT4 during the forward pass and upcast during backward
It trades communication bandwidth for memory — offload activations to other GPUs in the data-parallel group during forward
questions.explanation: Gradient checkpointing stores only the input to each checkpoint segment during the forward pass. When backward needs the intermediates (to compute parameter gradients), it reruns the segment forward from the saved input. The tradeoff: extra FLOPs (~33% more for naive full checkpointing, ~5% for selective) in exchange for activation memory dropping from O(L·A) to roughly O(√L · A) under the optimal spacing rule.

questions.question: Naive full checkpointing (one checkpoint per layer) adds 33% FLOP overhead. What is the exact source of this overhead, and why is it ~33% rather than ~100%?
options: The 33% comes from loading activations back from CPU; it is not 100% because PCIe bandwidth limits the transfer rate
The 33% comes from gradient accumulation being run twice — once for parameters and once for activation gradients
The 33% comes from a normalization pass over the checkpointed inputs that verifies numerical stability before backward
A normal step costs 1 fwd + 2 bwd = 3 units; checkpointing adds 1 extra fwd (recompute) per step = 4 units total. Overhead = (4−3)/3 = 33%
questions.explanation: Backward pass costs roughly 2× a forward pass. A normal step: 1 fwd + 2 bwd = 3 fwd-equivalents. With full checkpointing you recompute every layer's intermediates during backward, adding 1 extra forward: 1 fwd + 1 recompute + 2 bwd = 4 units. Overhead = 1/3 ≈ 33%. It is not 100% because recompute only adds one forward to a step that already costs three forward-equivalents.

questions.question: Selective checkpointing (Korthikanti 2022) achieves under 5% FLOP overhead yet saves nearly as much activation memory as naive full checkpointing. What is the key asymmetry it exploits?
options: The attention softmax output grows quadratically (O(L²)) and dominates activation memory at long sequences; recomputing only this component gets most of the memory savings for ~15% of a layer's FLOPs
Selective checkpointing drops only the last quarter of layers where gradients are largest and memory most constrained
ReLU/GELU intermediates can be recomputed for free because their binary mask is stored as a single bit per value
Selective checkpointing offloads FFN activations to CPU while keeping only the attention activations on GPU
questions.explanation: Activation volume per attention layer includes the softmax output of size B×L×L×heads — this grows quadratically with sequence length and dominates total activation memory at long contexts. FFN and linear-projection activations grow only linearly (B×L×d). Selective checkpointing recomputes only the attention softmax (the expensive-to-store O(L²) part) and keeps the cheap linear activations. Recompute FLOPs: ~15% of a layer → overhead drops from 33% to ~5%.

questions.question: Chen et al. 2016 derived the classical optimal checkpoint spacing as every √L layers for an L-layer network. What two quantities does this spacing minimise, and what does the √L rule achieve for each?
options: It minimises parameter memory and gradient variance; at √L spacing each checkpoint segment has equal weight and variance
It minimises total FLOPs and communication overhead; √L segments each of √L layers balances recompute with tensor-parallel all-reduce cost

It minimises activation memory and recompute FLOPs simultaneously; with √L checkpoints each spanning √L layers, memory cost is O(√L · A) and recompute cost is O(√L) extra forwards — both scale with √L giving a balanced minimum
It minimises GPU memory fragmentation and host memory usage; the √L spacing matches standard allocator page sizes
questions.explanation: With L layers and checkpoint spacing k: memory cost ≈ (L/k)·A (stored segment inputs) + k·A (the active segment's intermediates); recompute cost ≈ (L/k)·(k-1) layer recomputes per backward ≈ (L-k) extra forwards. Setting k=√L makes both terms scale as √L · A and L/√L = √L extra forwards, so no tradeoff is wasted. This is the original Chen 2016 sublinear-memory result.

questions.question: Step 3 of the Build code's model_backward_checkpointed re-runs the full segment forward for each saved input before computing gradients. For a 32-layer model segmented at k=4 (8 segments), how many total layer forward passes does one full backward call execute?
options: 8 passes: one mini-forward per segment, each processing 1 checkpoint boundary layer
40 passes: 8 segment recomputes × 4 layers each = 32 recompute layers plus the original 8 boundary layers
32 passes: the backward recomputes all 32 layers once each, equivalent to a second full forward pass
32 recompute passes (8 segments × 4 layers) — one full extra forward worth of FLOPs, consistent with the 33% overhead model
questions.explanation: Each of the 8 segments reruns 4 layers (model_forward from saved_inputs[seg_idx] processes params[start:end], length 4). That is 8×4 = 32 layer recomputes in backward. One full forward pass also processes 32 layers. So the recompute adds exactly one forward pass — the '33% overhead' formula (1 fwd + 1 recompute + 2 bwd) / (1 fwd + 2 bwd) − 1 = 33%.

questions.question: The doc warns against checkpointing the attention kernel when FlashAttention is already in use. Why does adding a checkpoint around an attention layer that uses FlashAttention add little benefit?
options: FlashAttention stores all its intermediates in HBM for fast retrieval, so the checkpoint has nothing to save
FlashAttention's backward kernel is faster than a full recomputed forward, making the checkpoint slower than just using backward directly
FlashAttention computes in BF16 while the checkpoint stores in FP32, causing precision mismatch that degrades gradient quality
FlashAttention's backward pass already internally recomputes the attention softmax on-chip; wrapping it in a checkpoint would recompute the softmax twice for no additional memory saving
questions.explanation: FlashAttention's backward kernel is designed around recomputation: it stores only the softmax log-sum-exp scalars (O(N) not O(N²)) and recomputes the full attention matrix during backward. Adding an outer activation checkpoint on the same layer would trigger another recompute of what FlashAttention already recomputes internally — double the work, zero extra memory saved. The doc says 'FlashAttention already recomputes the softmax fast, so additional layer-level checkpointing adds little on top.'
